title: How I deal with FOMO, and why I am not backing the new Cthulhu Death May Die and Wander campaigns
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q4mgxHWrmLM

Daniel (@dungeondive) answers a recurring question — why he doesn't back the big crowdfunding juggernauts — and uses the incoming campaigns for Cthulhu: Death May Die and Wander's *Cult of Barnacle Bay* as worked examples.

Three filters. (1) **Space.** A small house, a smaller hobby room, and a collection trending toward zines, solo RPGs, and smaller games. Every big box must displace something; going all-in on a CMON or Awaken Realms campaign produces a stack of boxes he has nowhere to put. ISS Vanguard's footprint alone is his cautionary tale. (2) **Time.** He can't give one game dozens of sessions, so highly narrative, multi-box campaign games are an automatic pass. (3) **Self-knowledge.** He has learned — especially in the last two years — that he dislikes rigidly scripted narrative campaigns and prefers character-driven campaigns he paces himself (Secrets of the Lost Tomb, Warhammer Quest, Shadows of Brimstone).

The two specific declines are not rejections of the games themselves — he loves both. What he wishes Monolith-er-CMON and Panda Cult would offer is *small-box expansions*: a season-three booster for Death May Die, or a card-only enemy-variety pack for Wander. He'll pick up pieces at retail and skip the new core boxes. Rising pledge prices and shipping are doing the FOMO-killing work for him anyway.

----

Should publishers lean harder into small-box expansions for beloved big-box games, or is the standalone-reboot model locked in by crowdfunding economics?
